perturbation.original_text: COOL TECH and VET TECH agree not to engage in any attempt whatsoever, to hire, or to engage as independent contractors, the other's employees or independent contractors during the term of this Agreement and for a period of six (6) months following expiration or termination of this Agreement except as may be mutually agreed in writing.
perturbation.changed_text: COOL TECH and VET TECH agree not to engage in any attempt whatsoever, to hire, or to engage as independent contractors, the other's employees or independent contractors during the term of this Agreement and for a period of two (2) years following expiration or termination of this Agreement except as may be mutually agreed in writing.
perturbation.explanation: Extending the non-solicitation period to two years is potentially problematic under Missouri law, which disfavors overly broad or lengthy non-compete/non-solicitation agreements. While this is a non-solicitation clause rather than a non-compete, the extended duration raises concerns about reasonableness.
perturbation.law_citation: Mayer Hoffman McCann, P.C. v. Barton, 614 F.3d 893 (8th Cir. 2010)
perturbation.law_explanation: Missouri courts scrutinize non-compete agreements (and by extension, non-solicitation agreements) to ensure they are reasonable in scope, geography, and duration. While there's no statutory limit for non-solicitation, a two-year period is longer than what courts often uphold, particularly if it unduly restricts employee mobility or is broader than necessary to protect legitimate business interests. The Mayer Hoffman McCann case illustrates the application of Missouri's reasonableness standard for restrictive covenants.
perturbation.justification: The original contract specified a six-month non-solicitation period post-termination, while the modified contract extends this to two years. The Perturbation Explanation suggests this extension might be problematic under Missouri law. The Law Explanation elaborates that Missouri courts scrutinize non-compete (and by extension, non-solicitation) agreements for reasonableness, particularly in duration. While no scraped snippets are provided, the generated explanation and perturbation explanation, coupled with the significant increase in the restricted period, indicates that the changed text creates a potential legal contradiction by potentially exceeding what Missouri courts deem reasonable, thus risking unenforceability.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of the State of Missouri. Without giving effect to its choice of law principles.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of the State of California. Without giving effect to its choice of law principles.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the governing law to California could impact the enforceability of the non-solicitation clause, as California law is generally more restrictive regarding such agreements than Missouri law. This could render the non-solicitation clause unenforceable.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Business and Professions Code Section 16600
perturbation.law_explanation: California Business and Professions Code Section 16600 generally prohibits contracts that restrain anyone from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business. While there are narrow exceptions, California courts have interpreted this broadly, making it difficult to enforce non-compete and non-solicitation agreements that restrict employee mobility. Switching the governing law to California makes the non-solicitation agreement in Article VIII highly vulnerable to being deemed unenforceable.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, BPC 16600 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Business and Professions Code - BPC DIVISION 7. GENERAL BUSINESS REGULATIONS [16000 - 18107] ( Division 7 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 61. ) PART 2. PRESERVATION AND REGULATION OF COMPETITION [16600 - 17365] ( Part 2 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) CHAPTER 1. Contracts in Restraint of Trade [16600 - 16607] ( Chapter 1 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) 16600. (a) Except as provided in this chapter, every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void. (b) (1) This section shall be read broadly, in accordance with Edwards v. Arthur Andersen LLP (2008) 44 Cal.4th 937, to void the application of any noncompete agreement in an employment context, or any noncompete clause in an employment contract, no matter how narrowly tailored, that does not satisfy an exception in this chapter. (2) This subdivision does not constitute a change in, but is declaratory of, existing law. (c) This section shall not be limited to contracts where the person being restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business is a party to the contract. (Amended by Stats. 2023, Ch. 828, Sec. 1. (AB 1076) Effective January 1, 2024.)
perturbation.justification: The changed text modifies the governing law from Missouri to California. According to the Law Explanation, California law, specifically Business and Professions Code Section 16600, generally prohibits contracts that restrain anyone from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business. Scraped Law Snippet 1 explicitly states in BPC 16600(a) that "every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void." The Perturbation Explanation indicates this change would likely render the non-solicitation clause unenforceable under California law. This constitutes a legal contradiction because the original contract, under Missouri law, presumably allowed for a more enforceable non-solicitation agreement. Switching to California law directly contradicts that enforceability, thus creating a legal contradiction.
